1:
"And the lot of the children of Joseph fell from Jordan by Jericho, unto the water of Jericho on the east, to the wilderness that goeth up from Jericho throughout mount Bethel," The witnesses note the lot of the children of Joseph, the portion of the favoured son fulfilled in his sons — the crux of the inheritance of the birthright, the blessing of Joseph realised in Ephraim and Manasseh. On "the lot of the children of Joseph fell from Jordan by Jericho... throughout mount Bethel," interpreters expound the lot. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the portion of Joseph — "and the lot of the children of Joseph fell from Jordan by Jericho, unto the water of Jericho on the east, to the wilderness that goeth up from Jericho throughout mount Bethel": the children of Joseph, Ephraim and Manasseh, received their lot, the portion of the favoured son; the lesson — that God honoured his promise to Joseph, the dreamer sold into Egypt, now exalted in his sons to a double inheritance, the providence that turned his affliction to honour, "God meant it unto good," Genesis 50:20; the truth that the blessing of Jacob upon Joseph, "a fruitful bough... whose branches run over the wall," Genesis 49:22, was fulfilled in the spreading inheritance of Ephraim and Manasseh; the figure — the reward of the faithful sufferer exalted, Joseph a type of Christ, sold and yet exalted to give bread and inheritance to his brethren. The figure of the inheritance of the birthright, the blessing of Joseph realised in Ephraim and Manasseh.

On "and the lot of the children of Joseph fell from Jordan by Jericho, unto the water of Jericho on the east," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the lot. Keil and Ellicott note the children of Joseph receiving their lot from Jordan by Jericho, the portion of the favoured son assigned (the inheritance of Joseph, 1 Chronicles 5:2) — the lot (of Joseph).

On "to the wilderness that goeth up from Jericho throughout mount Bethel," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the border. Calvin and Gill note the border of Joseph's portion running through mount Bethel, the blessing of Jacob upon Joseph fulfilled in the spreading inheritance, Joseph a type of Christ exalted (the blessing realised, Genesis 49:22; Genesis 50:20) — the figure (the reward of the faithful sufferer exalted); the providence (the lot, the border of Ephraim to follow, vv.2-4). The verse displays the lot of the children of Joseph.

The witnesses agree the substance: the children of Joseph, Ephraim and Manasseh, received their lot, the portion of the favoured son (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the inheritance of the birthright: the lot of Joseph — the inheritance of the son who received the birthright forfeited by Reuben, realised in his two tribes, the double portion of the firstborn (1 Chronicles 5:2), God honouring his promise to Joseph the dreamer, who was sold into Egypt and exalted (Genesis 50:20), the blessing of Jacob, "a fruitful bough," fulfilled in the spreading inheritance (Genesis 49:22), Joseph a type of Christ sold and yet exalted. The verse displays the birthright-inheritance crux. The lot of the children of Joseph displays the crux: the lot of the children of Joseph fell from Jordan by Jericho... throughout mount Bethel (1 Chronicles 5:2; Genesis 50:20; Genesis 49:22) — the inheritance of the birthright, the portion of the favoured son realised in Ephraim and Manasseh, the double portion of the firstborn, the blessing of Jacob fulfilled, Joseph a type of Christ exalted to give inheritance to his brethren. The verse displays the border of Ephraim from Bethel to the sea (vv.2-5).

The verse holds together the border.

10:
"And they drave not out the Canaanites that dwelt in Gezer: but the Canaanites dwell among the Ephraimites unto this day, and serve under tribute." The witnesses note that Ephraim drove not out the Canaanites of Gezer, but put them to tribute — the crux of the incomplete obedience that leaves the enemy in the land, the compromise with the world that becomes a snare. On "they drave not out the Canaanites that dwelt in Gezer: but the Canaanites dwell among the Ephraimites unto this day, and serve under tribute," interpreters expound the failure. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the incomplete obedience — "and they drave not out the Canaanites that dwelt in Gezer: but the Canaanites dwell among the Ephraimites unto this day, and serve under tribute": Ephraim failed to drive out the Canaanites of Gezer as God commanded, but spared them for tribute; the lesson — that incomplete obedience, sparing what God commands to be destroyed, leaves a snare in the midst of God's people, the Canaanites a thorn that would later seduce them to idolatry, "they shall be as thorns in your sides, and their gods shall be a snare unto you," Judges 2:3; the truth that the world spared for gain becomes a trap, the sin tolerated a corruption, the compromise that seems profitable proving ruinous; the figure — the besetting sin spared, the corruption left in the heart that should be wholly put off, "mortify therefore your members which are upon the earth," Colossians 3:5. The figure of the incomplete obedience that leaves the enemy in the land, the compromise with the world that becomes a snare.

On "and they drave not out the Canaanites that dwelt in Gezer," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the failure. Keil and Ellicott note Ephraim failing to drive out the Canaanites of Gezer as commanded, the full obedience left undone (the incomplete obedience, Deuteronomy 20:17-18) — the failure (drave not out).

On "but the Canaanites dwell among the Ephraimites unto this day, and serve under tribute," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the compromise. Calvin and Gill note the Canaanites spared for tribute, left dwelling among Ephraim, the enemy tolerated for gain becoming a snare to seduce them to idolatry (the compromise with the world, Judges 2:3; Colossians 3:5) — the figure (the besetting sin spared, the corruption left that should be put off); the providence (the failure, the inheritance of Manasseh to follow, ch.17). The verse displays Ephraim's failure to drive out the Canaanites of Gezer.

The witnesses agree the substance: Ephraim failed to drive out the Canaanites of Gezer, but spared them for tribute (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of incomplete obedience: the Canaanites not driven out — the failure of full obedience, the enemy left in the land contrary to God's command, spared for the gain of tribute rather than destroyed (Deuteronomy 20:17-18), the Canaanites a snare that would seduce God's people to idolatry, a thorn in their sides (Judges 2:3), the world spared for gain becoming a trap, a figure of the besetting sin tolerated, the corruption left that should be wholly mortified (Colossians 3:5). The verse displays the incomplete-obedience crux. Ephraim's failure to drive out the Canaanites of Gezer displays the crux: they drave not out the Canaanites that dwelt in Gezer: but the Canaanites dwell among the Ephraimites unto this day, and serve under tribute (Deuteronomy 20:17-18; Judges 2:3; Colossians 3:5) — the incomplete obedience that leaves the enemy in the land, the Canaanites spared for tribute against God's command, the compromise with the world that becomes a snare, a figure of the besetting sin tolerated that should be wholly put off. The verse opens the inheritance of Manasseh, the other son of Joseph (ch.17).
